schools.summary: Paul gathers up the promises just given (6:16-18 — God dwelling among them, being their Father) as the motive: 'having these promises,' the believer is moved to holiness; the call to 'cleanse ourselves' from 'all filthiness (defilement, pollution) of the flesh and spirit' — both outward, bodily sins and inward, spiritual ones, the whole person purified; 'perfecting (carrying on to completion) holiness in the fear of God' (Barnes, Henry, Robertson).

Wesley (with Calvin): 'perfecting holiness' is pressing toward full sanctification, the whole man cleansed; 'in the fear of God,' from reverent awe of him — the motive and atmosphere of all true holiness.

themes: let us cleanse ourselves from all filthiness of the flesh and spirit
perfecting holiness in the fear of God
the call to cleanse from all defilement
perfecting holiness

schools.summary: Paul pleads 'receive us' — make room for us in your hearts, give us your full affection and confidence (an appeal for reconciliation and openness); and defends his integrity against the slanders of the false teachers: he has 'wronged' (injured) no one, 'corrupted' (led astray, in doctrine or morals) no one, 'defrauded' (cheated of money) no one — a threefold disavowal (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Chrysostom): Paul's clear conscience is the ground of his appeal; having done them no harm, he may justly ask to be received with affection.

themes: Receive us; we have wronged no man, we have corrupted no man, we have defrauded no man
Paul's appeal to be received
his clear conscience
the threefold disavowal

schools.summary: Paul guards his words: 'I speak not this to condemn you' — his vindication (v.2) is not meant as a reproach or accusation against them; he does not wish to wound. The Corinthians are 'in our hearts' — so dear that he is ready 'to die and live with' them, bound to them in life and death, an expression of the most devoted love (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): Paul's whole appeal flows from love, not censure; he would share life and death with them, so deeply are they in his heart.

themes: I speak not this to condemn you
ye are in our hearts to die and live with you
Paul's deep love, not condemnation
bound in life and death

schools.summary: Paul pours out his feeling: 'great is my boldness (freedom, openness) of speech toward you' — he speaks to them frankly — and 'great my glorying (boasting) of you' — he boasts of them to others; he is 'filled with comfort' and 'exceeding joyful' (overflowing with joy), and this 'in all our tribulation,' his joy in the Corinthians' repentance overriding his many sufferings (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: the good news of the Corinthians' change (brought by Titus, v.6) so filled Paul with joy that it triumphed over all his troubles.

themes: Great is my boldness of speech toward you, great is my glorying of you
I am exceeding joyful in all our tribulation
Paul's boldness, glorying, comfort, and joy
joy triumphing over affliction

schools.summary: Paul recounts his distress: when he came into Macedonia (seeking Titus and news of Corinth), 'our flesh had no rest' — no relief of body or mind — 'but troubled on every side'; the distress twofold — 'without' (from outward foes, conflicts, persecutions) 'were fightings,' and 'within' (in his own heart) 'were fears' — anxiety about the Corinthians and the cause, pressed from without and within (Barnes, Henry, Robertson).
Calvin: Paul does not hide his weakness and fears, but freely confesses his anxiety — showing that even the apostle was subject to deep distress before God's comfort came.

themes: our flesh had no rest, but we were troubled on every side
without were fightings, within were fears
Paul's restlessness and trouble in Macedonia
pressed from without and within

schools.summary: The turn ('nevertheless'): 'God, that comforteth those that are cast down (the depressed, the humbled)' — a title of God as the consoler of the dejected — 'comforted us by the coming of Titus'; God's comfort came through Titus's arrival, the long-awaited return of his colleague with news, God using the human means to relieve Paul's distress (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: God is the comforter of the downcast, and he ministers his comfort often through the coming of friends and fellow-workers; Paul ascribes the relief to God, though it came by Titus.

themes: God, that comforteth those that are cast down, comforted us by the coming of Titus
God comforting the cast down
comfort by the coming of Titus
consolation through a friend

schools.summary: Paul's comfort came 'not by his coming only, but by the consolation wherewith he (Titus) was comforted in you' — Titus himself had been refreshed among them, and his report doubled Paul's joy; Titus told of their 'earnest desire' (longing to see Paul, or to set things right), their 'mourning' (grief over their fault), and their 'fervent mind (zeal) toward me' — tokens of their renewed love and repentance (Barnes, Robertson).
Wesley (with Calvin): this report of the Corinthians' longing, grief, and zeal made Paul 'rejoice the more' — his joy overflowing at their changed hearts.

themes: the consolation wherewith he was comforted in you
your earnest desire, your mourning, your fervent mind toward me
the comfort of Titus's good report
tokens of renewed love and repentance

schools.summary: Paul's frank disclosure: his former letter (the severe one, 1 Corinthians or a lost letter) 'made them sorry'; he had for a time 'repented' (regretted) sending it, fearing it too harsh, but now 'does not repent' — seeing its good effect; the sorrow it caused was 'but for a season' (temporary, transient), not a lasting wound but a passing grief that led to repentance (Barnes, Robertson, Chrysostom).
Calvin: Paul's momentary regret shows his tender heart, but the outcome justified the severity; the brief sorrow worked a lasting good.

themes: though I made you sorry with a letter, I do not repent, though I did repent
the same epistle hath made you sorry, though it were but for a season
Paul's reflection on the sorrow his letter caused
brief sorrow working lasting good

schools.summary: Paul carefully clarifies: 'I rejoice, not that ye were made sorry' (he takes no pleasure in their pain) 'but that ye sorrowed to repentance' — his joy is in the result, their turning, not the grief itself; the sorrow was 'after a godly manner' (according to God's will), the right kind that leads to repentance and life; and they received 'damage in nothing,' his letter doing them only good (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: the sorrow was according to God, the kind that leads to life; Paul rejoices not in the wound but in the healing it brought.

themes: I rejoice... that ye sorrowed to repentance
ye were made sorry after a godly manner
joy not in sorrow but in repentance
godly sorrow doing no harm

schools.summary: 'Godly sorrow' (sorrow according to God — that regards sin as committed against God, and leads the soul to him) 'worketh (produces) repentance' — a true change of mind and life (metanoia, not mere regret), 'to salvation,' and this repentance is 'not to be repented of' (gives no cause for regret, is permanent); while 'the sorrow of the world' (grief from worldly causes — loss, shame, fear of consequences, not from sin against God, the remorse of Cain, Esau, and Judas) 'worketh death' — despair and ruin (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

Calvin: godly sorrow is the cause and origin of repentance — no one returns to God but the man who hates sin, and where hatred of sin is, there is self-dissatisfaction and grief; yet repentance is the way to salvation, not its ground, for God pardons by free mercy and never without repentance — the two are inseparable fruits of grace, lest works be made the cause of justification.

themes: godly sorrow worketh repentance to salvation not to be repented of
the sorrow of the world worketh death
the contrast of godly and worldly sorrow
saving repentance vs deadly remorse

schools.summary: Paul appeals to the Corinthians' own case as proof that godly sorrow produces real change, enumerating seven fruits: 'carefulness' (earnest diligence to set things right), 'clearing of yourselves' (apologia — vindication, eagerness to be rid of blame), 'indignation' (against the sin), 'fear' (of God's displeasure, or of Paul's rod), 'vehement desire' (longing to amend or to see Paul), 'zeal' (fervent earnestness), and 'revenge' (ekdikesis — just punishment of the offender); by which they 'approved themselves clear in this matter,' having done all they could to remove the evil (Barnes, Clarke, Robertson).

Calvin: these are the signs and accompaniments of repentance, springing from self-dissatisfaction; by punishing the sin themselves the Corinthians anticipated God's judgment (1 Corinthians 11:31) — not to make satisfaction to God, but as evidence of a true and open repentance for an open sin.

themes: what carefulness it wrought in you, yea, what clearing of yourselves... zeal... revenge
ye have approved yourselves to be clear in this matter
the seven fruits of godly sorrow
the evidences of true repentance

schools.summary: Paul states his deeper purpose: he wrote his severe letter not merely for the sake of the offender (the wrongdoer) nor the one wronged (the injured party), but for a larger end — that his 'care (earnest concern) for you' (for the whole church's good and purity) might be made manifest, and this 'in the sight of God,' sincerely and before him (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).
Calvin: Paul's discipline aimed not at individuals merely but at the welfare of the entire church; his severity flowed from pastoral love for them all.

themes: I did it not for his cause that had done the wrong, nor for his cause that suffered wrong
that our care for you in the sight of God might appear
Paul's true aim in writing
care for the whole church

schools.summary: Paul 'was comforted in your comfort' — the Corinthians' own consolation (their being set right) was his comfort — and he rejoiced 'exceedingly the more for the joy of Titus,' because Titus's 'spirit was refreshed by you all'; the warm reception and changed conduct of the Corinthians had revived and gladdened Titus, and Paul's joy was multiplied by his colleague's joy (Barnes, Robertson, Chrysostom).
Calvin: the mutual joy of apostle, colleague, and church shows the bond of love among them; Paul rejoices the more because Titus too was gladdened.

themes: we were comforted in your comfort
exceedingly the more joyed we for the joy of Titus, because his spirit was refreshed by you all
Paul's added joy at the refreshing of Titus
the mutual joy of apostle, colleague, and church

schools.summary: Paul had 'boasted' to Titus of the Corinthians (before sending him), and now 'is not ashamed' — for his boast proved well-founded; their conduct vindicated his confidence; and his integrity is consistent — just as all he had spoken to the Corinthians was true, so his boasting of them to Titus was likewise found true, his word reliable in both directions (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: Paul's honesty is consistent; he neither flattered the Corinthians nor misrepresented them to Titus, and the event proved his word trustworthy.

themes: if I have boasted any thing to him of you, I am not ashamed
our boasting, which I made before Titus, is found a truth
Paul's boasting of the Corinthians proved true
his trustworthy word

schools.summary: Titus's 'inward affection (tender love, literally his bowels, the seat of affection) is more abundant toward you' — his love for the Corinthians had grown, deepened by his experience among them; he remembered 'the obedience of you all' (their ready compliance) and how they 'received him with fear and trembling' (with deep reverence, anxious care to do right, humble respect for his message) — and this won his heart (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: the Corinthians' humble, obedient reception of Titus and his message drew out his abundant love; reverent obedience binds hearts together.

themes: his inward affection is more abundant toward you
the obedience of you all, how with fear and trembling ye received him
Titus's deepened affection for the obedient Corinthians
reverent obedience binding hearts

schools.summary: Paul closes the section with joy: 'I rejoice therefore that I have confidence (full trust, good courage) in you in all things' — his confidence in them, once shaken, is now fully restored; 'in all things,' not in some matters only but comprehensively, their repentance and obedience having won back his entire confidence; and this restored confidence becomes the ground on which he proceeds, in the next chapters, to urge the collection for the saints (Barnes, Robertson).

themes: I rejoice therefore that I have confidence in you in all things
Paul's restored confidence in the Corinthians
full trust regained
the ground for the appeal to follow
